title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates

title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: useful diagnostic plot observed study-level treatment effects covariate overlay estimated regression line uncertainty:  points naive study-level estimates, solid line posterior median regression 90% credible band, dashed line true data-generating relationship.
code: # Compute naive normalised effect per study naive_effects <- did_data |>   mutate(     norm = mean_pre_control,     naive_effect = (mean_post_treatment - mean_pre_treatment) / norm -                    (mean_post_control - mean_pre_control) / norm   )  # Posterior draws for regression line beta_draws <- as.numeric(   fit$fit$draws("beta_cov", format = "draws_matrix") ) intercept_draws <- as.numeric(   fit$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") )  # Covariates were centered; reconstruct on original dose scale dose_center <- fit$cov_centers[["dose"]] dose_grid <- seq(min(dose), max(dose), length.out = 100)  # Each draw gives a line: intercept + beta * (dose - center) line_df <- expand.grid(   dose = dose_grid,   draw = seq_along(beta_draws) ) |>   mutate(     fitted = intercept_draws[draw] + beta_draws[draw] * (dose - dose_center)   ) |>   group_by(dose) |>   summarise(     median = median(fitted),     lo     = quantile(fitted, 0.05),     hi     = quantile(fitted, 0.95),     .groups = "drop"   )  # True regression line on the normalised scale true_line_df <- data.frame(dose = range(dose)) |>   mutate(true_effect = (-0.15 + -0.04 * dose) / 0.45)  ggplot() +   geom_ribbon(data = line_df, aes(x = dose, ymin = lo, ymax = hi),               alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = dose, y = median)) +   geom_line(data = true_line_df, aes(x = dose, y = true_effect),             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(data = naive_effects, aes(x = dose, y = naive_effect),              alpha = 0.6) +   labs(x = "Dose", y = "Normalised treatment effect") +   theme_minimal()

Sometimes covariate better described scaling effect — multiplying factor rather shifting . common motivating example: experimental (“artificial”) settings often produce larger effect sizes real-world deployments, ’d like estimate much real-world effect attenuated relative experimental one, jointly experimental effect . metadid supports multiplicative_covariate argument. covariate categorical reference level: one multiplier estimated per non-reference level applied studies level, reference level’s factor fixed 1. two-level indicator mi∈{0,1}m_i \\{0, 1\} simplest case, single estimated multiplier applied studies equals 1: μi={μθ+Xcov,⊤βcovmi=0multiplier⋅(μθ+Xcov,⊤βcov)mi=1.\mu_i = \begin{cases} \mu_\theta + X_{\mathrm{cov},}^{\top}\beta_{\mathrm{cov}} & m_i = 0 \\ \mathrm{multiplier} \cdot \bigl(\mu_\theta + X_{\mathrm{cov},}^{\top}\beta_{\mathrm{cov}}\bigr) & m_i = 1. \end{cases} Studies mi=0m_i = 0 identify linear predictor directly; studies mi=1m_i = 1 see multiplied multiplier. multiplier strictly positive, log-normal lognormal(0, 0.7) prior: median 1 (-effect case, every study contributes population mean) boundary zero, small attenuating multiplier pushed hard limit. prior placed log⁡(multiplier)\log(\mathrm{multiplier}), factor symmetric “halving” versus “doubling”.

title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: build illustrative summary data frame 30 studies three settings (10 ): experimental batch two real-world batches whose true effects attenuated relative . study tagged setting. first pass collapses two real-world settings single {0, 1} indicator, estimating one multiplier “real-world” relative experimental: reference level (0, experimental) fixed 1. interpretation: experimental studies produce effect around -0.41, real-world studies produce roughly half (multiplier ≈ 0.50). combination yields per-study mean used meta-analysis.
code: make_batch <- function(effect, setting, seed) {   sim <- simulate_meta_did(     n_studies = 10, n_control = 100, n_treatment = 100,     true_effect = effect, sigma_effect = 0.05,     true_trend = 0, baseline_mean = 0.45, seed = seed   )   sim$study_id <- paste0(setting, "_", sim$study_id)  # keep IDs unique across batches   out <- as_summary_did(sim)   out$setting <- setting   out }  studies <- rbind(   make_batch(-0.41, "experimental", 11),   make_batch(-0.27, "rw_lab",       22),   make_batch(-0.14, "rw_field",     33) ) studies$real_world <- ifelse(studies$setting == "experimental", 0, 1)  fit <- meta_did(   summary_data             = studies,   multiplicative_covariate = "real_world",   priors = set_priors(     multiplier = lognormal(0, 0.7)   ),   seed = 9931 )  print(fit) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 30 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0 #> Population treatment effect: -0.410  90% CI [-0.450, -0.372] #> Multiplicative covariate (real_world): #>   0: 1  (reference) #>   1: 0.503  90% CI [0.412, 0.598]

title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: single fit may carry two multiplicative covariates. Pass one-sided formula naming columns; estimated independently study’s overall multiplier product two per-covariate factors, αa()⋅βb()\alpha_{()} \cdot \beta_{b()}. suits design effect scaled two distinct study attributes — example intervention delivered, crossed intensively applied. factor’s reference level (in_person delivery, high intensity, alphabetically first) fixed 1, rows report remaining levels. remote delivery retains 62% -person effect low-intensity programme 55% high-intensity one; remote, low-intensity study scaled product (≈ 0.62 × 0.55 ≈ 0.34). two covariates summary rows prefixed covariate name disambiguate . two multiplicative covariates supported.
code: # Two independent study attributes, each scaling the effect multiplicatively. studies$delivery  <- rep(c("in_person", "remote"), length.out = nrow(studies)) studies$intensity <- rep(c("high", "low"),         length.out = nrow(studies))  fit <- meta_did(   summary_data             = studies,   multiplicative_covariate = ~ delivery + intensity,   priors = set_priors(multiplier = lognormal(0, 0.7)) ) summary(fit) #>                             parameter   mean    sd     lo     hi #> 1               treatment_effect_mean -0.410 0.020 -0.443 -0.377 #> 2                 treatment_effect_sd  0.055 0.011  0.039  0.075 #> 3  effect_multiplier[delivery:remote]  0.620 0.082  0.498  0.761 #> 4   effect_multiplier[intensity:low]  0.550 0.073  0.441  0.679

what: When to use a multiplicative vs additive covariate
title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: two parameterisations encode different beliefs world: Use additive study’s effect shifted fixed amount linked covariate, shift size regardless underlying effect’s magnitude. Use multiplicative covariate attenuates amplifies underlying effect — e.g. real-world implementation captures fraction experiment showed, stronger experimental effect also produce proportionally stronger real-world effect. choice matters prediction unobserved covariate values: multiplicative model, predicted real-world effect tracks experimental effect’s posterior; additive model differs fixed offset. multiplicative covariate can combined one additive covariates fit — provided collinear , flagged .

title: Model details and identification
text: metadid assumes every study — regardless design — arises common latent difference--differences () data-generating process. study ii, outcomes control group follow (Yi,c,preYi,c,post)∼𝒩[(αiαi+βi),(σi,c,pre2ρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postσi,c,post2)], \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \alpha_i + \beta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right], outcomes treatment group follow (Yi,t,preYi,t,post)∼𝒩[(αi+γiαi+γi+βi+θi),(σi,t,pre2ρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postσi,t,post2)]. \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i + \gamma_i \\ \alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right]. parameters : αi\alpha_i: baseline mean control group βi\beta_i: time trend shared across groups γi\gamma_i: baseline difference treatment control groups θi\theta_i: study-specific treatment effect (estimand) ρi,c\rho_{,c}, ρi,t\rho_{,t}: pre/post correlations within group σi,g,t\sigma_{,g,t}: marginal standard deviations key identifying assumption , absent treatment, treatment group followed time trend βi\beta_i control group (.e., parallel trends assumption).

title: Model details and identification
text: Different study designs observe different subsets latent bivariate structure: design sees fewer cells, less ability separate parameters α\alpha, β\beta, γ\gamma, θ\theta.

title: Model details and identification
text: studies observe four cells 2×2 table. mean structure , four expected cell means : Taking double difference expected values gives (μi,t,post−μi,t,pre)−(μi,c,post−μi,c,pre)=(βi+θi)−βi=θi. (\mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}) - (\mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}) = (\beta_i + \theta_i) - \beta_i = \theta_i. time trend βi\beta_i cancels, baseline difference γi\gamma_i cancels, leaving θi\theta_i cleanly identified. studies anchor entire model.

what: DiD (change only) studies: identified but without levels
title: Model details and identification
text: studies report mean change time (post minus pre) arm, without reporting level means separately. expected change scores βi\beta_i (control) βi+θi\beta_i + \theta_i (treatment), difference change scores still identifies θi\theta_i. However, pre-treatment levels observed, studies anchor normalisation baseline (see ).

what: RCT studies: treatment effect confounded with baseline differences
title: Model details and identification
text: RCT studies observe post-treatment outcomes arms. expected difference arms μi,t,post−μi,c,post=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+βi)=γi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \beta_i) = \gamma_i + \theta_i. Without pre-treatment data, θi\theta_i confounded baseline difference γi\gamma_i. Randomisation makes γi\gamma_i small expectation, model separate two data single RCT alone.

what: Pre-post studies: treatment effect confounded with time trends
title: Model details and identification
text: Pre-post studies observe treatment arm time points. expected change time μi,t,post−μi,t,pre=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+γi)=βi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \gamma_i) = \beta_i + \theta_i. Without control arm, θi\theta_i confounded time trend βi\beta_i.

title: Model details and identification
text: Without studies, treatment effect identified data. meta_did() stop error studies present. check can overridden allow_no_did = TRUE, resulting posterior driven primarily priors rather data. studies present alongside RCT pre-post studies, hierarchical model propagates information: time trend distribution estimated studies informs pre-post decomposition, baseline structure studies informs RCT decomposition. cross-design borrowing core value metadid approach.

title: Model details and identification
text: RCT pre-post designs, model uses differenced-form likelihoods eliminate nuisance parameters algebraically: RCT: likelihood based treatment–control difference post-treatment means, time trend βi\beta_i cancels. remaining parameters treatment effect θi\theta_i baseline difference γi\gamma_i (borrowed studies). Pre-post (default): likelihood based within-subject post–pre difference, baseline αi+γi\alpha_i + \gamma_i cancels. remaining parameters treatment effect θi\theta_i time trend βi\beta_i (borrowed studies). pre-post studies, non-differenced (bivariate normal) form available via meta_did_general(pp_likelihood = "bivariate"). retains pre/post correlation ρi\rho_i estimable parameter, contributing hierarchical ρ\rho model, cost estimating additional nuisance parameters.

title: Model details and identification
text: default (normalise_by_baseline = TRUE), meta_did() normalises outcome data design-appropriate baseline mean fitting. places studies common relative scale, making treatment effect interpretable proportional change.

title: Model details and identification
text: pre-post studies, normalisation divides pre-treatment quantity, relevant baseline parameter known exactly 1 fixed rather estimated. time trend treatment effect remain free parameters. change-studies report change scores (post minus pre) rather level means, lack study-specific pre-treatment level normalise . text: design_effects = TRUE, model allows population treatment effect mean differ systematically design: μθ,RCT=μθ+δRCT,μθ,PP=μθ+δPP \mu_{\theta,\text{RCT}} = \mu_\theta + \delta_{\text{RCT}}, \quad \mu_{\theta,\text{PP}} = \mu_\theta + \delta_{\text{PP}} δRCT\delta_{\text{RCT}} δPP\delta_{\text{PP}} estimated offsets. relaxes assumption designs estimate exactly estimand, may appropriate selection effects time trends differ systematically across designs.

title: Model details and identification
text: meta_did_general() function provides explicit control nuisance parameters handled non-designs, via three arguments: "pooled" (default): hierarchical prior shared across designs, informed studies. "fixed_zero": βi=0\beta_i = 0 RCT pre-post studies. pre-post studies, attributes pre-post change treatment. RCTs, bypasses time trend reparameterisation described . "estimated" (default): γi\gamma_i estimated, information borrowed studies normalised. "fixed_zero": γi=0\gamma_i = 0, assuming randomisation eliminates baseline imbalances. "differenced" (default): uses post-minus-pre difference, eliminating baseline algebraically. pre/post correlation ρi\rho_i separately estimable. "bivariate": uses full bivariate normal likelihood (pre, post) pair. retains ρi\rho_i estimable parameter, contributing hierarchical ρ\rho model, cost estimating additional nuisance parameters. settings can combined independently. example, one might trust randomisation assumption (baseline_imbalance = "fixed_zero") still borrowing time trend information studies (time_trend = "pooled"). default settings meta_did_general() identical meta_did(). Another third RCTs (post-treatment outcomes ), remaining third pre-post studies (treatment arm ):
code: all_did <- as_summary_did(sim)  fit_all_did <- meta_did(   summary_data = all_did,   seed         = 495 )  print(fit_all_did) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 50 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0 #> Population treatment effect: -0.332  90% CI [-0.348, -0.314] study_ids <- unique(sim$study_id) true_params <- attr(sim, "true_params")  sim_did <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[1:17]) sim_rct <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[18:34]) sim_pp  <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[35:50])  attr(sim_did, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[1:17]) attr(sim_rct, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[18:34]) attr(sim_pp, "true_params")  <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[35:50])  mixed <- bind_rows(   as_summary_did(sim_did),   as_summary_rct(sim_rct),   as_summary_pp(sim_pp) )  fit_mixed <- meta_did(   summary_data = mixed,   seed         = 495 )  print(fit_mixed) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 17 | RCT = 17 | Pre-Post = 16 | DiD (change only) = 0 #> Population treatment effect: -0.330  90% CI [-0.349, -0.311]

what: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
text: Deprecated. meta_did_naive() deprecated favour meta_did_general() time_trend = "fixed_zero" baseline_imbalance = "fixed_zero", provides behaviour explicit control. function imposes "naive" assumptions typically made analysing design isolation: RCT: Baseline means assumed equal across treatment control groups (randomisation assumption), baseline difference \(\gamma\) fixed zero. Pre-post: time trend \(\beta\) fixed zero, pre-post change attributed entirely treatment effect.

title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
code: meta_did_naive(   summary_data = NULL,   individual_data = NULL,   normalise_by_baseline = TRUE,   robust_heterogeneity = FALSE,   design_effects = FALSE,   hierarchical_rho = TRUE,   covariates = NULL,   multiplicative_covariate = NULL,   center_covariates = TRUE,   priors = set_priors(),   method = c("sample", "optimize"),   chains = 4L,   iter_warmup = 1000L,   iter_sampling = 1000L,   seed = NULL,   allow_no_did = FALSE,   ... )

title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
text: summary_data data frame one row per study containing summary statistics. Must include columns study_id design. See validate_summary_data() full column specification per design. Valid designs: "", "did_change", "rct", "pp". individual_data data frame long format one row per observation. Must include columns study_id, design, group, time, value. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp". study_id may appear summary_data individual_data. normalise_by_baseline Logical. TRUE (default), means SDs divided study's pre-treatment control mean (grand mean change-studies), placing outcomes common fractional scale. reported treatment_effect_mean population mean per-study proportional effects, \(E[\theta_i / b_i]\) (percentage-scale effect, study expressed fraction baseline), appropriate estimand studies heterogeneous scales. Note \(E[\theta] / E[b]\): baselines vary across studies two differ -study baseline coefficient variation squared (Jensen's inequality). robust_heterogeneity Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects drawn Student-t distribution rather normal, providing robustness outlier studies. degrees--freedom parameter estimated prior specified priors$nu. design_effects Logical. TRUE, additive offsets population treatment effect mean estimated RCT Pre-Post studies relative (reference). Useful testing whether designs yield systematically different effect estimates. hierarchical_rho Logical. TRUE (default), pre-post correlation modelled hierarchically across studies. Studies reported correlation inform population distribution; studies without one correlation imputed. covariates optional one-sided formula specifying study-level covariates meta-regression treatment effect (e.g., ~ dose + year). named columns must numeric present summary_data individual_data (whichever provided). individual-level data, covariate values must constant within study. Default NULL (meta-regression). multiplicative_covariate Optional specification one two categorical study-level covariates modify population treatment effect multiplicatively rather additively. Either single column name (character length 1) one covariate, one-sided formula naming one two columns (~ ~ + b). two allowed. One factor estimated per non-reference level covariate: studies covariate's reference level keep population-mean linear predictor \(\mu_\theta + X_{\mathrm{cov},}^{\top}\beta_{\mathrm{cov}}\) unchanged (factor fixed 1), studies level \(k\) multiplied estimated effect_multiplier[k]. two covariates study's overall factor product two per-covariate factors, \(\alpha_{()} \cdot \beta_{b()}\) — .e. covariate scales effect independently (log-additive structure). reference level first factor level (declare column factor control , identical levels declared every data frame), lowest value numeric input, alphabetically first value character input. numeric {0, 1} indicator simplest case: 0 reference (factor fixed 1) 1 selects single estimated multiplier. Useful study attribute attenuates amplifies underlying effect shared factor — e.g. intervention delivered, optionally crossed second attribute long ran. column must contain NAs, constant within study individual-level data, must also appear covariates, must take least two distinct values across studies multipliers identified; two columns must distinct. Numeric columns 5 distinct values rejected likely continuous (convert genuinely categorical numeric codes factor). multiplier prior set_priors() applied independently every estimated factor. returned object, fit$multiplicative_covariate list elements name levels (reference first) one covariate, list two descriptors two covariates. Default NULL (multiplicative structure). center_covariates Logical. TRUE (default), covariates mean-centered across studies fitting. ensures treatment_effect_mean population treatment effect average covariate values. Set FALSE use raw covariate values, case treatment_effect_mean effect covariates equal zero. covariate coefficients (beta_cov) interpretation regardless centering: change expected treatment effect per unit increase covariate. priors did_priors object set_priors(). Controls prior distributions population-level parameters. method Inference method. "sample" (default) runs full MCMC via Stan's HMC-NUTS sampler returns posterior distribution. "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS substantially faster, returns point estimate uncertainty quantification. chains Number MCMC chains. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 4. iter_warmup Number warmup iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. iter_sampling Number sampling iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. allow_no_did Logical. FALSE (default), meta_did() stop error studies present, treatment effect identified data without double-difference structure. Set TRUE override check understand limitation (posterior prior-driven). ... title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
text: treatment_effect_mean Prior population treatment effect mean. Default: normal(0, 10). treatment_effect_sd Prior -study SD. Default: cauchy(5). time_trend_mean Prior population time-trend mean. Default: normal(0, 10). time_trend_sd Prior -study time-trend SD. Default: cauchy(5). rho_mean Prior mean Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 1). rho_sd Prior SD Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 0.5). nu Prior degrees freedom -study heterogeneity (used robust_heterogeneity = TRUE). Default: gamma(2, 0.1). delta_rct Prior RCT design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10). delta_pp Prior Pre-Post design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10). sigma Prior study-level observation standard deviations (shared across designs). Default: cauchy(5). beta_cov Prior covariate regression coefficients (used covariates specified meta_did()). Default: normal(0, 10). lkj_eta Prior Cholesky factor correlation matrix treatment effects time trends (used correlated_effects = TRUE). Default: lkj(2), gently regularises toward zero correlation. baseline_difference_mean Prior population mean per-study baseline imbalance (treatment-arm vs control-arm pre-treatment mean, normalised fractional scale). used baseline_imbalance = "estimated". Default: normal(0, 0.5). baseline_difference_sd Prior -study SD baseline imbalance. used baseline_imbalance = "estimated". Default: cauchy(0.1). multiplier Prior multiplicative-covariate effect multiplier (used multiplicative_covariate specified meta_did()). one two multiplicative covariates prior applied independently every estimated non-reference-level factor (either covariate). Must lognormal() prior, placed log multiplier strictly positive boundary zero. Default: lognormal(0, 0.7) — median 1 (-multiplicative-effect case), central 95% range roughly [0.25, 3.9] natural scale.

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: n_studies Number studies. Default 20. n_control Number individuals per study control arm. Default 100L. n_treatment Number individuals per study treatment arm. Default 100L. true_effect Population mean treatment effect. Default -0.15. sigma_effect -study SD treatment effects. Default 0.03. true_trend Population mean time trend. Default -0.02. sigma_trend -study SD time trends. Default 0. baseline_mean Population mean study baselines. Default 0.45. baseline_sd -study SD baselines. Default 0. within_sd Individual-level SD (pre post groups). Default 0.12. rho Pre-post correlation within individuals. Directly parameterises -diagonal bivariate normal covariance matrix. Default 0.5. rho_effect_trend Correlation study-level treatment effects \(\theta_i\) time trends \(\beta_i\). nonzero (sigma_effect sigma_trend positive), pair drawn jointly bivariate normal. Default 0 (independent draws). seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. covariates optional data frame n_studies rows containing study-level covariate values. Column names used covariate names. Default NULL (covariates). beta_cov numeric vector true covariate regression coefficients, length equal ncol(covariates). Required covariates provided. coefficient represents change treatment effect per unit increase corresponding covariate. Default NULL.

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: data frame columns study_id, subject_id, group ("control" "treatment"), time ("pre" "post"), value, plus covariate columns (repeated observation within study). true study-level parameters attached attribute "true_params".

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: Study-level parameters drawn hierarchically: $$\theta_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_effect},\, \texttt{sigma\_effect}^2)$$ $$\beta_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_trend},\, \texttt{sigma\_trend}^2)$$ $$b_i      \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{baseline\_mean},\, \texttt{baseline\_sd}^2)$$ rho_effect_trend nonzero, \((\theta_i, \beta_i)\) drawn jointly bivariate normal correlation rho_effect_trend. Within study, individual (pre, post) pairs drawn bivariate normal distribution covariance matrix $$\Sigma = \sigma^2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ making role \(\rho\) pre-post correlation explicit. mean vector control group \((b_i,\, b_i + \beta_i)\) treatment group \((b_i,\, b_i + \beta_i + \theta_i)\).
